sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE]
There were apparently people in those camps who were artists or writers or had different professions.
And without any books or without any tools, they gave lectures.
And you know, my mother attended lectures on literature.

There were people that had-- could sing, and she attended, quote, unquote, 'concerts.' My father took courses in electrical engineering, in photography without any tools, without cameras, without anything.

And this was all in-- wanting to keep their minds alert and to keep their minds off the-- probably the difficult life that they were leading and to prepare them for, you know, another profession maybe, that maybe my father could become a photographer or an electrician or something like that.
And I think that is a form of resistance, because you could-- I'm sure there were people who just sat in a corner and vegetated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the-- in July of 1942, my mother was sent from Camp de Gurs to Camp de Rivesaltes.
It's C-A-M-P, new word, D-E, new word, capital R-I-V-E-S-A-L-T-E-S, which was sort of halfway between Gurs and Les Milles.
And the amazing thing is a letter that she wrote to me after she arrived in Rivesaltes.
And she-- and to me, it was like a travel log.
And I thought, wow, she's taking a wonderful trip.
I wish I could have been on that trip.

She talks about getting a glimpse of the grotto in Lourdes and said-- told me how indescribably beautiful it is, and that she was coming-- the train that she was on was coming past fields of weaving corn or wheat and so on, that she saw tropical fruits on the trees, oranges and bananas, got a glimpse of the blue Mediterranean.
I mean, what presence of mind to write a letter like that to me.
And so I thought, you know, life is great, because they never complained.

And then I got a letter from my father dated August 9th, 1942, in which he said tomorrow, I'm going to be deported to an unknown destination and it may be a very long time before you hear from me again.

And then I received a letter from my mother, dated September 1st, 1942, in which she makes reference to the fact that my father was-- had been deported and that she did not know where he was and had not heard from him, and that she was not deported at the same time as my father was, because he lately had been a forced laborer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but that now there was another transport leaving the following day and that she was leaving on that transport and that she was hoping to be reunited with my father somewhere, somehow, and that they would then carry their burden or their life.

And that was perhaps the first time that she was complaining with dignity and with courage, and she's asking me to be always honest, to be brave and good, to carry my head high, and to never forget them and to never forget any of all of us.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then there was one more postcard from my mother, dated September 4, 1942, which I suspect was written on the train or whatever other mode of transportation that she was on.
And I suspect she probably tossed it out and some kind soul picked it up and mailed it to me.
And it was written in real shaky handwriting.
And she's saying that she's traveling to the east and she's saying a very final goodbye to me.
But until many, many years later, I really chose that postcard to say or interpreted that postcard to say something very different, that she was traveling in an easterly direction.
And I thought well, Kippenheim, the village where we lived is in an easterly direction and maybe she's going back home.
Maybe that's a good idea.
And where she's saying a very final goodbye, I thought it was the kind of goodbye you say when you go on a trip and, you know, hope to see you again soon.
And, you know, both my parents had said it may be a very long time before you hear from us again.
And how long is a long time?
Is it a week, a month, a year?
And I just kept on telling myself a long time just isn't over yet.
And I think in many ways, it was probably a survival mechanism on my part, because I don't think I could have accepted the fact that my parents are no longer alive.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then in-- and so I just kept on waiting, you know, until the long time will finally be over, because I fervently hoped and wished that I would be reunited with them again somewhere, somehow.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then in 1956, I received two letters from a French organization, telling me that both my parents had been deported to the concentration camp Auschwitz, A-U-S-C-H-W-I-T-Z, on September 11th, 1942.
And by 1956 when these letters arrived, I certainly knew a lot more about what Auschwitz was about.
And I knew also that there were a handful of people who survived.
And so I continued to still hope to be reunited with my parents somewhere.
And in 1983, I learned that my mother indeed was sent to Auschwitz on September 11th, 1942.
My father was already sent there on August 19th, 1942.
And all my other family members who were in Gurs or some of the other camps in France, they all between August and September 1942 were sent to Auschwitz.
None of them have ever been heard from again.
The only one that I was able to find out-- find out some information about is my father's youngest brother, Oskar.
I understand he arrived in Auschwitz on August 12th 1942 and died there on I believe it was August 27th 1942, of some intestinal problems allegedly.
I only got that information a couple years ago.
The Russians released some information about Auschwitz, and I requested information about my parents and everyone else.
And that's the only information I was able to obtain.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1980, I went back to Europe to visit most of the camps that my parents had been to.
I went to Gurs, to Dachau, and the last place was Auschwitz.

And when I stood on the ramp in Auschwitz where when the trains arrived and people got off the trains and the selection was made as to who will live a few-- maybe live a few more days or weeks and be put to work because they were young enough and strong enough and the rest were sent to the gas chambers because they were too old, had young children, or were too sick, when I stood on that ramp in September 1980 I finally accepted the fact that my parents indeed did not survive.
You know, we go through rituals when people die.
You usually know when and why the person died.
You may see the body.
There's a funeral.
There's a tombstone.
I don't have any of those things.
And I think that, combined with the fact that I needed to deny it to be able to continue my life, I-- that's probably why it took me that long to accept the fact that they're no longer alive.
Life was difficult.
I stayed with the cantors in the cantor's home only a short time, only about 10 weeks, because there were some problems there.
And I was then put in a or placed in a girls' home in Belsize Park.
That's B-E-L-S-I-Z-E Park, P-A-R-K, which is part of London.
The girls' home was run by a woman by the name of Mrs.
Glicksman, G-L-I-C-K-S-M-A-N, who was herself a refugee from Germany.
Miss Glicksman was not a very good person.
She was-- really exploited all of us.
No matter how much we earned, that's how much we owed her for our board and lodging.
And there were three buildings.
I was first placed in one of the buildings that had no heat in the winter.
And once the pipes froze, there was no water.
I mean, I literally had a crust of dirt on my body.
My clothes were grey because I couldn't wash them.
Later on I moved into one of the other buildings, the same building where she lived.
And there was heat and hot water there, or warm water there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I initially worked in the home.
She had what was called a doll factory.
We were making dolls and doll clothes.
Later on she branched on into making children's clothes.
And by 1942, I think, or 1943, another girl and I decided we were going to branch out and we're going to go and work someplace else.
And we both worked for Harrods department store in London.
We were making children's dresses.
It was the same department that made the clothes for now Queen Elizabeth and her sister Margaret, but we didn't get to make those clothes.
And then I think in late 1943 I decided that I really needed to do something for the war effort.
And I worked-- changed jobs and worked in a war factory.
I worked in a couple of war factories until the end of the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- also, while I lived in this girls' home, there were-- again, you know, I mentioned in the beginning that I felt different.
And I felt different there.
In my spare time, I attended a college and I took courses in literature and psychology, not towards a degree, but just to-- for my own edification.
And a lot of the girls in this home were interested in I guess finding a husband.
And the American army was there, so they went to the dances to meet GIs.
Some of the girls became-- I guess, because they probably came from very good homes, but they were so desperate for love some of them actually became prostitutes.
One of them encouraged me to join her, but I resisted that.
There was a lot of stealing going on.
And some of it was really not to get maybe something of value, because I remember one of my roommates had photographs of her parents stolen.
It was, you know, I think, someone searching for love.
Maybe she didn't have pictures of her parents.
There was suicide.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a German Jewish organization, or mostly Jewish, that had its headquarters across the street.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a lot of animosity towards that group.
And perhaps because of that I thought, I have to find out.
And I went across the street.
And it's-- and I joined the organization.
And it's there that I really got my political education, which has made me who I am today.
One of the goals of that organization was to go back to Germany after the war and reeducate the German population in democratic principles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, it was concerned about both the emotional and psychological well-being of people, but also to become actively involved in doing things.
And that has really been part of my life ever since.
I think it's the lesson that I've taken from the Holocaust.
You know, many people or many survivors say, you know, why wasn't more done for us?
Well, there are people in this world, in this city today who are suffering.

And knowing what suffering is-- and mine certainly wasn't the worst, because I was never in a concentration camp-- I think it behooves me, it's my duty to become involved in the issues whereby I can maybe make a difference.
I'm not going to be able to change the world.
But I think each and every one of us not only can but must make a difference.
And I don't think any one of us is insignificant enough to not be able to do this, maybe very small things.
And I frequently talk to students.
And I always urge them and challenge them to become involved in no matter what it is.
Maybe something in their school.
Maybe they see two students fighting.
They have a choice to make.
They can walk past and ignore it, or maybe they can intervene.
And it may seem it's a small thing, but it's-- we all need to do that to make this world a better world, not only for others, but in the process for ourselves also.
Right.
Yes.
I did go back to Germany in July of 1945, not as I had originally planned, to re-educate the Germans, but I was fortunate enough to obtain employment with the American government.
I worked for the US Civil Censorship division for one year in Munich, Germany, censoring incoming and outgoing German mail.
And my intention was when my contract is over, my one-year contract is over, I will then stay in Germany as I had planned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- that's not what happened, though.
When the train stopped in Germany the first time as I was returning, there were children on the platform begging for chewing gum and candy, cookies, and whatever.
And there were people on the train just like I, refugees from Germany, Austria, Czechoslovakia, going back to Germany to work for the American government.
And some of them gave these children whatever they had.
And I just became absolutely livid.
How dare you give something to these Nazis?
And some of these so-called Nazis were, you know, five years old maybe.
But I could-- I-- I was not aware until that moment that I had such anger and such hatred within me.
And I was not able to rationalize that these children are not guilty.
They've not done anything.
And it took me quite a while before I was able to get over this hatred for everybody who was German.
And the begging by the children continued, because Germany was in pretty bad shape.
And I was then finally able to give children whatever they were asking for or whatever I had.
But they had to eat it right in front of me, could not take it home, because at home there might be a Nazi.
And cigarettes, no, you're too young to smoke and you can't take them home.
But the hatred across the board for the adult German population stayed with me for all too long.
And I finally was able to rid myself of that hatred in the spring of 1970.
I-- at that time, the United States was involved in the Vietnam War.
And I had been against that war probably before there was even an anti-Vietnam war movement.
And I learned in late April or early May 1970 that as part of the Vietnam War, the United States had been carpet-bombing Cambodia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it-- I just was enraged that we had done this.
But it also triggered some thoughts in my mind.
And I was saying, you know, I picketed and I marched and I wrote letters and sent telegrams to my congressional people, to the president.
And it didn't endanger my life or that of my family.
And then my thoughts traveled across the ocean and across the years back to Germany.
And I was saying, now, if the Germans had done what I did, they would have been sent to a concentration camp or there would have been some dire consequences for them.
And there was resistance in Germany, but most of those people unfortunately did not survive.
And so I was saying, you know, how can I condemn a whole nation of people for not being heroes?
I don't know whether I would risk my life.
Fortunately, I've never been put to that task.
And so with that, the hatred across the board against the adult German population, it just-- it's vanished.
It's gone.
In fact, in 1991, I was in Germany on a speaking tour.
And after one of my presentations, a couple of people told me afterwards, you're much too forgiving of Germans.
And I said to them, you know, I don't know you.
Why should I hate you?
And even if you've done some despicable thing, let's talk about it, why you did it.
Let me understand why.
I know I didn't finish one trend of thought.
After my contract with the Censorship division expired, or even prior to that, I applied-- I decided I wanted to stay in Germany longer.

And I applied for a position with the Nuremberg Trial, with the-- not with the international trial, but with the-- what was called the subsequent proceedings, which were conducted by the American government in the same Palace of Justice in Nuremberg where the international trial took place.
And there were 12 such trials.
And I was assigned to the trial of the doctors who performed medical experiments on concentration camp inmates.
And my job was to look for the documentary evidence used in the trial.
And I would spend most of my time in Berlin in a former Nazi document center, which was located in Dahlem, D-A-H-L-E-M, which is a suburb of Berlin.
This document center was very deep under the ground.
And it was operated by the American Army.
And there was an American colonel by the name of Helms, H-E-L-M-S, who was in charge of that document center.
He was born in Germany and had come to the United States I believe in the 1920s.
But his sympathies were still with the Nazis.
And most of us working there were former German Austrian refugees, Jewish, most of us.
And he hated us with a passion, probably because we were Jewish.
And he made life really difficult for us.
There were two ways of going down or coming back up from the document center.
One was stairs and the other was an elevator.
And soon after we got there he had the stairs bricked shut so we only had-- could use the elevator.
And he had-- upstairs, he had control of the power.
And frequently, the power was shut off so we could not get out.
But we also had no light.
The ventilation system was off.
But we very quickly learned how we could function despite all that.
We brought candles.
And so it was not romantic, but we did our work by candlelight.

We complained to Nuremberg about this, but Nuremberg told us that this center, this document center, is run by the Army, the American Army, and they have no control over the Army, so do what you can and make the most of it.
And we did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I left-- the trial, you know, at some point came to an end.
And so I left in March of 1948 and went back to England.
I'm sorry?
Well, it was very, very difficult to read how those experiments were conducted.
And there was detailed information about or descriptions how they were conducted.
And there were many, many times when I couldn't eat, or if I did eat, I vomited.
I couldn't sleep.
At one point I came across a document.
It was actually a list of people who were going to be deported to Auschwitz.
And I looked through that list, even though that was not what I was actually looking for as far as my work was concerned.
And I found my father's name on that list.
The list was dated January 1942, but I discounted it, because I still heard from my father as late as August 9th, 1942.
And so I-- I don't know whether that list really was later on used when my father was deported.
That I don't know.
But I just discounted it at that time.
And I think-- you know, I discounted a whole lot of things that should have told me what happened to my parents, because I just could not accept that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When-- I had the opportunity sometimes when I was in Nuremberg to sit in on the court sessions, especially the trial of the doctors who performed the medical experiments.
And I remember one young woman who had been experimented on who had to be carried into the court room because she could no longer walk.

The experiment that was conducted on her was a rusty knife had been used to cut open the fleshy part in the lower part of both of her legs and some foreign objects had been inserted, like rusty nail, dirt, or whatever.
And then it was sewn up again.

And the doctor, a woman who performed this experiment, woman by the name of Herta, H-E-R-T-A, Oberheuser, O-B-E-R-H-A-U-S-E-R, who was on trial and sitting in the docket, had performed this experiment on this young woman.
Most of the people who had this-- this kind of experiment conducted on them did not survive.
They suffered from blood poisoning, which was not treated, and then they died.
The young woman was asked, do you see anyone in this courtroom who has performed this experiment on you?
And she pointed to Herta Oberheuser.
Herta Oberheuser had absolutely no-- a blank expression on her face, like this had nothing whatsoever to do with her.
She subsequently was sentenced to 20 years in prison, got out after 10 years, and practiced as a pediatrician, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I find just almost incredible.

The purpose for that experiment was because the Nazis rationalized why they were doing these awful experiments was that soldiers get wounded and lie someplace in the field and dirt is in their wounds and they lie there for a while before they treat it and it becomes infected and what happens?
That's true.
But you know, if indeed, the wound was later on cleaned out and sewn up and then began to fester or get-- become infected, it would be opened up to see what's wrong.
And if there was something, some foreign object left inside, you remove it.
You don't-- didn't have to conduct those experiments.
And you know, there were many, many other horrible experiments that were conducted, hypothermia experiments, high altitude tests, malaria, I mean, just any number of tests.
And each one the Nazis rationalized why they conducted them.
I didn't see you being--
